title: SQL
description: Practice the query patterns that actually show up in interviews and analytics take-homes.

reviewer.summary: If you know almost no SQL, the fastest path is to memorize the shape of common queries and always reason from rows: what rows exist now, what rows do I want next, and what clause changes that shape.
interviewFocus: Can you write the basic query skeleton from memory?
Can you explain how joins and grouping change row shape?
Can you use examples to reason instead of guessing syntax?
Can you solve variants of familiar patterns by changing only a few clauses?

sections.title: Must Know In Time For Interviews
points: Memorize the standard order of thinking: FROM, JOIN, WHERE, GROUP BY, HAVING, SELECT, ORDER BY, LIMIT.
Practice joins and aggregates heavily because most interview SQL mistakes come from wrong row shape.
Learn a few reliable templates for joins, GROUP BY, subqueries, CTEs, and windows, then adapt from them.

sections.title: Suggested Crash-Course Sequence
points: Start with SELECT, WHERE, ORDER BY until the syntax feels automatic.
Then learn joins, then GROUP BY and aggregates, then subqueries and CTEs.
Only after that spend serious time on window functions, schema design, and indexing.

sections.title: What Usually Fails People
points: Writing syntax from memory without checking whether the rows make sense.
Forgetting that joins can duplicate rows before aggregation.
Grouping at the wrong grain or selecting columns that do not match that grain.

chapters.title: SELECT / WHERE / ORDER BY
chapters.focus: Filter and shape result sets correctly.

chapters.title: Joins
chapters.focus: Combine tables without dropping or duplicating rows by accident.

chapters.title: GROUP BY & Aggregates
chapters.focus: Summarize data at the right grain.

chapters.title: Subqueries
chapters.focus: Use nested filtering and derived sets.

chapters.title: CTEs
chapters.focus: Break long queries into readable steps.

chapters.title: Window Functions
chapters.focus: Rank, partition, and compute rolling stats.

chapters.title: Schema Design
chapters.focus: Pick stable table structure and keys.

chapters.title: Indexes & Tuning
chapters.focus: Know what makes a query fast or expensive.

questions.prompt: You want each employee's name and salary. Which query shape is correct?
questions.codeSnippet:
-- table: employees(id, name, salary, team)
-- goal: show name and salary only

choices.label: SELECT name, salary FROM employees;
SELECT employees INTO name, salary;
FROM employees SELECT name salary;
GET name, salary FROM employees;

questions.explanation: The baseline SQL template is SELECT columns FROM table. Start there and only add clauses when the problem needs them.

questions.prompt: You only want employees in the 'Sales' team, ordered by salary descending. Which clauses do you need?
questions.codeSnippet:
-- table: employees(id, name, salary, team)
-- goal: only Sales rows, highest salary first

choices.label: WHERE team = 'Sales'
ORDER BY salary DESC
GROUP BY salary
HAVING team = 'Sales'

questions.explanation: WHERE filters rows before they are returned. ORDER BY controls display order. GROUP BY and HAVING are for grouped results, not simple row filtering.
questions.studyNote:
Common filter + sort template

SELECT name, salary
FROM employees
WHERE team = 'Sales'
ORDER BY salary DESC;

questions.prompt: You want the 5 most recent orders by created_at. What should the query end with?
questions.codeSnippet:
-- table: orders(id, customer_id, created_at)
-- goal: newest 5 orders only

choices.label: ORDER BY created_at DESC LIMIT 5
ORDER created_at LIMIT 5 DESC
GROUP BY created_at DESC LIMIT 5
WHERE created_at DESC LIMIT 5

questions.explanation: To get the top N rows by some ordering, sort first and then limit the number of rows returned.
questions.studyNote:
Top N template

SELECT *
FROM orders
ORDER BY created_at DESC
LIMIT 5;

questions.prompt: You need every customer, even those with no orders. Which join is correct?
questions.codeSnippet:
-- customers(id, name)
-- orders(id, customer_id, total)
-- goal: list all customers with any matching order data if it exists

choices.label: FROM customers c LEFT JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id
FROM customers c INNER JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.customer_id
FROM customers c CROSS JOIN orders o
FROM customers c SELF JOIN orders o ON c.id = o.id

questions.explanation: LEFT JOIN keeps all rows from the left table and fills missing matches with nulls. That is the standard answer when the prompt says 'include even if none exist.'
questions.studyNote:
Keep all left-side rows template

SELECT c.name, o.total
FROM customers c
LEFT JOIN orders o
  ON c.id = o.customer_id;

questions.prompt: What is the main first-principles risk after joining a one-to-many table like customers to orders?
questions.codeSnippet: -- one customer can have many orders

choices.label: Rows can multiply, which can distort later counts and sums
SQL stops allowing ORDER BY after a join
Joined tables cannot use WHERE anymore
The database converts numbers into strings

questions.explanation: The most important habit in SQL is watching row shape. A one-to-many join often increases row count, which can make downstream aggregates wrong if you ignore it.
questions.studyNote:
Join first-principles question

After a join, ask:
- How many rows did I have before?
- How many rows do I have now?
- Did one row turn into many rows?

questions.prompt: Which join condition is correct for customers and orders?
questions.codeSnippet:
-- customers(id, name)
-- orders(id, customer_id, total)
-- choose the key relationship

choices.label: ON customers.id = orders.customer_id
ON customers.name = orders.id
ON customers.id = orders.total
ON customers.customer_id = orders.id

questions.explanation: Join keys should match the primary key of one table to the foreign key in the other. Here, orders.customer_id points to customers.id.
questions.studyNote:
Key-matching habit

Primary key -> foreign key
customers.id = orders.customer_id

questions.prompt: You want the number of orders per customer_id. Which query shape is correct?
questions.codeSnippet:
-- orders(id, customer_id, total)
-- goal: one row per customer_id with count of orders

choices.label: SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) FROM orders GROUP BY customer_id;
SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) FROM orders;
SELECT COUNT(customer_id) GROUP BY orders;
SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) FROM orders ORDER BY customer_id GROUP BY total;

questions.explanation: When you want one row per category, group by that category and aggregate the rows inside each group.
questions.studyNote:
Core aggregate template

SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*) AS order_count
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id;

questions.prompt: After grouping by customer_id, which kind of columns are safe to select?
questions.codeSnippet: -- grouped result: one row per customer_id

choices.label: The grouped column itself, like customer_id
Aggregate expressions like COUNT(*) or SUM(total)
Random ungrouped row-level columns like orders.id
AVG(total)

questions.explanation: A grouped query should only select columns that make sense at the new grain: group keys and aggregate results. Raw row-level columns usually violate that grain.
questions.studyNote:
Grain rule

After GROUP BY X:
- you may select X
- you may select aggregates
- you usually may not select raw row-level columns

questions.prompt: You only want customers with more than 3 orders. Which clause filters grouped results?
questions.codeSnippet: -- goal: keep only groups where COUNT(*) > 3

choices.label: HAVING COUNT(*) > 3
WHERE COUNT(*) > 3
ORDER BY COUNT(*) > 3
LIMIT COUNT(*) > 3

questions.explanation: WHERE filters raw rows before grouping. HAVING filters the grouped results after aggregates have been computed.
questions.studyNote:
Grouped filter template

SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*)
FROM orders
GROUP BY customer_id
HAVING COUNT(*) > 3;

questions.prompt: You want employees whose salary is above the company average. Which pattern fits best?
questions.codeSnippet:
-- employees(id, name, salary)
-- compare each row to the overall average salary

choices.label: WHERE salary > (SELECT AVG(salary) FROM employees)
GROUP BY salary > AVG(salary)
ORDER BY salary > (AVG salary)
JOIN AVG(salary) WITHOUT a subquery

questions.explanation: A subquery is useful when you need a single derived value, like the average salary, and then compare each outer row against it.
questions.studyNote:
Compare to overall aggregate template

SELECT name, salary
FROM employees
WHERE salary > (
    SELECT AVG(salary)
    FROM employees
);

questions.prompt: You want customers who have placed at least one order. Which subquery pattern works?
questions.codeSnippet:
-- customers(id, name)
-- orders(id, customer_id)
-- goal: customers whose id appears in orders.customer_id

choices.label: WHERE id IN (SELECT customer_id FROM orders)
WHERE id GROUP BY orders
WHERE id = COUNT(orders)
HAVING id IN orders

questions.explanation: IN subqueries are a common beginner pattern when you want values from one table that appear in a set produced by another query.
questions.studyNote:
Membership subquery template

SELECT id, name
FROM customers
WHERE id IN (
    SELECT customer_id
    FROM orders
);

questions.prompt: What is the first-principles reason to use a subquery?
questions.codeSnippet: -- think in terms of 'use one query's result inside another query'

choices.label: To compute an intermediate value or set that the outer query depends on
Because SQL requires every query to have one
To replace all joins automatically
Only to make the query shorter, never for logic

questions.explanation: The right mental model is simple: a subquery creates an intermediate value or set, and the outer query uses it.
questions.studyNote:
Subquery mental model

Inner query -> produces value or set
Outer query -> uses that value or set

questions.prompt: What is the main reason to use a CTE?
questions.codeSnippet:
-- CTE = Common Table Expression
WITH recent_orders AS (
    SELECT *
    FROM orders
    WHERE created_at >= '2025-01-01'
)
SELECT *
FROM recent_orders;

choices.label: To break a query into named, readable steps
Because CTEs are always faster than every other option
To replace SELECT entirely
Because GROUP BY only works inside CTEs

questions.explanation: The beginner value of CTEs is readability. They let you name an intermediate step and then build on it cleanly.
questions.studyNote:
CTE template

WITH step_name AS (
    SELECT ...
    FROM ...
    WHERE ...
)
SELECT ...
FROM step_name;

questions.prompt: You want to first filter recent orders, then aggregate them by customer. What is the clearest pattern?
questions.codeSnippet:
-- step 1: filter recent orders
-- step 2: count recent orders per customer

choices.label: Use a CTE for recent_orders, then aggregate from that CTE
Put GROUP BY before the filtering step
Use ORDER BY instead of filtering
Skip the intermediate step and hope the final query stays understandable

questions.explanation: When you naturally think in steps, a CTE often matches the way you want to reason about the problem.
questions.studyNote:
Filter then aggregate template

WITH recent_orders AS (
    SELECT *
    FROM orders
    WHERE created_at >= '2025-01-01'
)
SELECT customer_id, COUNT(*)
FROM recent_orders
GROUP BY customer_id;

questions.prompt: Which statement about CTEs is the safest beginner rule?
questions.codeSnippet: -- think of CTEs mainly as query organization tools

choices.label: They help you separate logic into named blocks
They can make complex queries easier to debug
They automatically fix bad join logic
They can make repeated logic easier to reuse in the same query

questions.explanation: CTEs help structure your work, but they do not magically correct wrong logic. You still need the row reasoning to be right.
questions.studyNote:
Use CTEs when you catch yourself saying:
- first get these rows
- then summarize them
- then rank or filter the result

questions.prompt: You want to rank employees by salary within each team. Which window function is the right starting point?
questions.codeSnippet:
-- employees(name, team, salary)
-- goal: rank salary within each team

choices.label: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY team ORDER BY salary DESC)
COUNT(*) OVER (ORDER BY team)
SUM(team) OVER (PARTITION BY salary)
LIMIT 1 OVER team

questions.explanation: Ranking within groups is one of the most common beginner window-function tasks. Partition defines the group; order defines the rank order.
questions.studyNote:
Rank within group template

SELECT name, team, salary,
       ROW_NUMBER() OVER (
           PARTITION BY team
           ORDER BY salary DESC
       ) AS team_rank
FROM employees;

questions.prompt: What does PARTITION BY do in a window function?
questions.codeSnippet: -- example: ROW_NUMBER() OVER (PARTITION BY team ORDER BY salary DESC)

choices.label: It restarts the window calculation separately for each group
It deletes duplicate rows before ranking
It is the same as WHERE
It sorts the final query output automatically

questions.explanation: PARTITION BY is the window equivalent of 'do this calculation separately within each group.'
questions.studyNote:
Mental model

PARTITION BY = split rows into groups
ORDER BY = define sequence inside each group

questions.prompt: You want a running total of daily revenue ordered by day. Which pattern is right?
questions.codeSnippet:
-- sales(day, revenue)
-- goal: running total by day

choices.label: SUM(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY day)
GROUP BY day SUM(revenue) OVER
LIMIT revenue ORDER BY day
WHERE SUM(revenue) BY day

questions.explanation: A running total is a classic window pattern: keep row detail, but add an accumulating sum ordered by time.
questions.studyNote:
Running total template

SELECT day, revenue,
       SUM(revenue) OVER (ORDER BY day) AS running_revenue
FROM sales;

questions.prompt: An order can contain many products, and a product can appear in many orders. What table design is needed?
questions.codeSnippet: -- think in terms of relationships between entities

choices.label: A join table such as order_items(order_id, product_id, quantity)
Put all product ids in one comma-separated string on orders
Duplicate the entire product table inside orders
Store product names only and skip ids

questions.explanation: Many-to-many relationships are normally modeled with a separate join table that stores the relationship rows.
questions.studyNote:
Many-to-many pattern

orders
products
order_items(order_id, product_id, quantity)

questions.prompt: What is the beginner-friendly purpose of a primary key?
questions.codeSnippet: -- example: customers(id, name, email)

choices.label: To uniquely identify each row in the table
To store the most important text column
To automatically sort the table forever
To replace all foreign keys

questions.explanation: A primary key gives each row a stable identity. That identity is then referenced by foreign keys in related tables.
questions.studyNote:
Basic schema identity rule

primary key -> identifies this row
foreign key -> points to another table's primary key

questions.prompt: Which design choice is usually stronger for customer and order data?
questions.codeSnippet: -- choose between normalized structure and repeated duplicated data

choices.label: Keep customers in one table and orders in another linked by customer_id
Copy full customer name and address into every order row only
Put customers and orders into one giant wide table
Store orders as one long text blob inside customers

questions.explanation: A normalized design usually stores entities once and connects them by keys instead of copying all attributes repeatedly into every related row.
questions.studyNote:
Basic normalization instinct

Customers are one entity.
Orders are another entity.
Link them with customer_id.

questions.prompt: Which query is the clearest candidate for an index on users(email)?
questions.codeSnippet: -- assume a large users table

choices.label: SELECT * FROM users WHERE email = 'a@b.com';
SELECT * FROM users;
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM users;
SELECT * FROM users ORDER BY RANDOM();

questions.explanation: Indexes help most when you filter on a column or small set of columns to find a subset of rows quickly.
questions.studyNote:
First-principles index rule

Indexes are most useful when the database needs to find matching rows quickly for filters or join keys.

questions.prompt: You often filter by customer_id and then order by created_at. Which index is the strongest starting guess?
questions.codeSnippet:
-- common query pattern:
-- WHERE customer_id = ?
-- ORDER BY created_at DESC

choices.label: INDEX(customer_id, created_at)
INDEX(total, notes)
INDEX(created_at, customer_id, total, notes, status, id)
No index could ever help that pattern

questions.explanation: A composite index is often useful when queries repeatedly filter by one column and then read rows in the order of another column.
questions.studyNote:
Composite-index instinct

If query pattern is:
WHERE A = ?
ORDER BY B

then a starting guess is often INDEX(A, B)

questions.prompt: Why is SQL tuning hard if you do not understand the row shape and query logic first?
questions.codeSnippet: -- tune only after the query is logically correct

choices.label: You might speed up a query that still returns the wrong answer
Performance work cannot replace understanding joins and grouping
Indexes automatically fix every bad query pattern
A wrong-grain query can stay wrong even if it runs faster

questions.explanation: Correctness comes first. Tuning a logically wrong query is just a faster way to get the wrong answer.
questions.studyNote:
Safe order of work

1. Get the row logic right
2. Get the result right
3. Then worry about performance
